feedback.text: I would like to propose to add the following point to the Digital Fairness Act: Practices that prevent consumers from using digital products they have purchased. This does not of course relate to monthly subscriptions, but to other software products. The classical example would be, for example, a video game, which requires a permanent online connection to servers and thus becomes completely unplayable once the servers in question are taken off-line. Publishers/developers should instead either install an offline functionality or provide an alternative to online game, for example by enabling the host of their own servers, or through a peer-to-peer connection system. There is already a demand for regulation, for example through the ‘Stop Killing Games’ Citizens’ Initiative, which collected over 1.4 million signatures over the last year. Such an act could also protect other digital products, such as books, audio games or videos acquired through an online service, and would be lost when that service is discontinued, unless users can access either an offline version of the service or a possibility to open the files in question by other means.
